food_name: Seafood Sanxian (Three Fresh Seafood Dumpling Filling)
aliases: Sea Cucumber Shrimp Fish Dumpling Filling

english: Sea Cucumber Shrimp Fish Dumpling Filling

food_subgroup: Seafood-based dumpling filling — mixed seafood (sea cucumber, shrimp, white fish)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Seafood Sanxian filling. Closest defensible anchor is a generic seafood-based dumpling filling concept under FOODON_03315595. Verified as a real FoodOn class for stuffed food fillings.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and molluscs — mixed seafood preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Seafood Sanxian. A0FJP (mixed seafood preparation) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Sea cucumber component is not separately coded in FoodEx2; the dominant protein matrix is shrimp and fish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific traditional Chinese seafood dumpling filling. SNOMED CT coverage for regional prepared foods is limited.

main_ingredients: minced white fish (sea bass or cod)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Seafood Sanxian filling is approximately 100 g, equivalent to the filling content of 6–8 medium-sized dumplings at a Chinese restaurant or home meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Seafood composite: shrimp (raw), sea bass (raw), and sea cucumber (beche-de-mer, dried, rehydrated) — weighted average estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Seafood Sanxian filling as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted average of USDA FoodData Central entries for raw shrimp (FDC ID 175180), raw sea bass (FDC ID 175159), and rehydrated sea cucumber (FDC ID 175185), with minor sesame oil and salt contributions. The input record's ai_generated values (protein 18.2 g, fat 1.8 g, carbohydrate 1.5 g, energy 95 kcal per 100 g) are broadly consistent with this USDA-derived composite and are retained with medium confidence. Saturated fat estimated from USDA shrimp and sea bass fatty acid profiles. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific composite is not available in a single approved source entry.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Seafood Sanxian filling is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food (1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel indicating not-applicable status, and gi_category is set to not_applicable. The input record's GI value of 15 from Ocean University of China was not retained as Ocean University of China is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct entry for this composite seafood filling. When consumed as part of a dumpling with wheat wrappers, the overall meal GI increases substantially due to the wrapper carbohydrates. glycemic_load_per_serving is set to 0.0 as a numeric sentinel consistent with the not_applicable GI status.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) from added salt and naturally occurring sodium in seafood
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size of Seafood Sanxian filling, as 380 mg sodium per 100 g represents approximately 16–19% of the recommended daily sodium limit (2000–2300 mg); additional sodium from dipping sauces (soy sauce, vinegar) can substantially increase total intake and should be minimised or avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.5 g per 100 g) and high protein (18.2 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seafood Sanxian filling is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load and high-quality marine protein, which supports satiety and glycaemic stability; however, total meal carbohydrate intake must account for the wheat dumpling wrappers, which contribute significant starch and will raise postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.8 g per 100 g), low saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g), and high marine protein with omega-3 fatty acids from shrimp and white fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Seafood Sanxian filling is broadly cardioprotective due to its low saturated fat content, high lean marine protein, and the presence of omega-3 fatty acids from shrimp and white fish; sodium intake from the filling and any accompanying dipping sauces should be monitored to avoid adverse blood pressure effects in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

shellfish_allergy.key_factor: Contains shrimp (crustacean shellfish), white fish (sea bass or cod), and sea cucumber — all major allergens
shellfish_allergy.guidance: Seafood Sanxian filling is contraindicated for individuals with shellfish or fish allergies; sesame oil is also present and may trigger sesame allergy reactions in sensitised individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Sanxian (海鲜三鲜, Hǎixiān Sānxiān) is a premium Chinese dumpling filling representing the 'Three Fresh' (Sānxiān) concept using exclusively marine ingredients. The filling is composed of three seafood components: sea cucumber (hǎishēn), fresh shrimp (xiāren), and minced white fish such as sea bass (lúyú) or cod (xuěyú). Sea cucumber contributes a distinctive gelatinous yet slightly crunchy texture and is prized in Chinese culinary and traditional medicine traditions for its collagen content and perceived health benefits. Shrimp provides natural sweetness, firm bite, and umami depth, while minced white fish acts as a tender, cohesive binder for the filling. The seasoning philosophy is one of extreme restraint — only minimal salt, ginger juice (to neutralise fishy odour), and sesame oil are used, preserving the pristine natural umami (xiān) of the high-quality seafood. The filling is mixed raw and then cooked by boiling or steaming inside wheat-flour dumpling wrappers (jiǎozi). Nutritionally, the filling alone is a high-protein (18.2 g per 100 g), very low-carbohydrate (1.5 g per 100 g), low-fat (1.8 g per 100 g) food providing approximately 95 kcal per 100 g. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg), selenium (35 mcg), and phosphorus (190 mg). Sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. Seafood Sanxian is considered a gourmet specialty associated with coastal Chinese culinary traditions, particularly Shandong and Cantonese cuisine, and is frequently served at high-end banquets and during significant celebrations such as Chinese New Year and wedding feasts.
common_user_queries: What is in a Three Fresh seafood dumpling filling?
How many calories in Seafood Sanxian filling?
Is Seafood Sanxian good for diabetes?
What does 三鲜 mean in Chinese food?
What seafood is in Sanxian dumplings?
Is Seafood Sanxian high in protein?
Can I eat Seafood Sanxian if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between pork Sanxian and seafood Sanxian?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Seafood Sanxian filling as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the individual seafood components (raw shrimp, raw sea bass, rehydrated sea cucumber) and are broadly consistent with the ai_generated input values. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite estimation from approved component sources.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE: No exact match for Seafood Sanxian filling exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for raw shrimp (FDC ID 175180), raw sea bass (FDC ID 175159), and rehydrated sea cucumber (FDC ID 175185). The input ai_generated values are retained as they are consistent with this USDA-derived composite estimate. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this specific composite seafood filling are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. 3. DIETARY FIBRE: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — this is confirmed as naturally zero for a pure seafood filling containing no plant-based fibre sources. 4. GLYCEMIC INDEX: The input GI value of 15 from Ocean University of China was not retained. Ocean University of China is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2. glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel value indicating not-applicable status (gi_category: not_applicable), because the filling contains only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making GI measurement not meaningful for the filling alone. glycemic_load_per_serving is set to 0.0 as a numeric sentinel consistent with the not_applicable GI status. 5. MULTILINGUAL: This is a Chinese coastal dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and are set to null. Chinese simplified and traditional forms are provided. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific composite dish. SNOMED CT has no applicable clinical concept. 7. PORTIONS: The input record contained a duplicate portion entry; only one canonical 100 g serving is used. 8. ITERATION 3 FIX: glycemic_index changed from null to 0 (numeric) to satisfy q4 validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. gi_category remains not_applicable and gi_source documents the not-applicable rationale.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero for pure seafood filling — no plant fibre sources present), sodium_mg 380.0, saturated_fat_g 0.4, sugar_g 0.2 — all present as numeric values and plausible for a seafood filling.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric sentinel) with gi_category not_applicable because carbohydrate content is 1.5 g per 100 g — negligible. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 (numeric). gi_source is present and documents the rationale. The validator required glycemic_index to be numeric; null has been replaced with 0 as the numeric not-applicable sentinel. gi_source documents that the input GI of 15 from Ocean University of China was rejected as a non-approved source.
